title: Authorization for Web Applications

Authorization for Web Applications

Authorization for Web Applications

Authorization ensures that users have appropriate privileges to perform actions or access resources.

Authorization is intended to

  • Ensure that only authorized users can perform allowed actions within their privilege level
  • Control access to protected resources using decisions based upon role or privilege level
  • Prevent privilege elevation attacks

In general, the web application should run at privilege level that is as low as possible. In case of attack, a web application that runs at a privilege level that is too high grants an attacker access to too many resources.

As a web developer, you can only control the privilege levels for the web application processes that run on the server side—the web server (such as Tomcat), Uniface Router, Uniface Server, and database.

Threats

  • Privilege escalation
  • Access to confidential data
  • Data manipulation
  • Execution of unauthorized commands

Guidelines

When developing a web application, consider the following guidelines:

  • Do not provide a static download link to generated documents. Instead, generate such content dynamically and send it directly to the client without storing it on the local file system.

    If static content is needed, ensure that access controls are in place.

    If you need to store files on the file system, use a randomly generated filename. For example, you can use the Uniface $uuid ProcScript function.

  • Do not use client-side authentication or authentication tokens, and do not set them in headers, cookies, hidden form fields, or the URL. Once a user is authenticated, use the associated session to maintain state in a session cookie, in hidden form fields, or in state tables (preferable).
  • Avoid copying or rewriting authorization checks. Instead, use an authorization component and call the component at appropriate points in the web application. Rather than writing your own authorization component, it is better to use a standard authorization component such as Java’s SAAS. Test coverage for authentication components should approach 100 percent.
  • In an access-controlled web application, you must check that users are allowed to view a page or use an action. This check needs to be done early (for example, in the preRequest trigger), and should comprise a check to a centralized authorization component.
  • Check that a user has authorization to not only perform an action, but also access a requested resource.

Example: Uniface and Tomcat Privileges on Windows

On the Microsoft Windows platform, the Tomcat web server is usually installed using the local system account, which has too many privileges. To avoid this:

  • Start the web server as a local service, with a low privileged account that is specifically allowed to logon as a service.
  • Only allow administrative users to have write permissions to the $CATALINA_HOME directory.
  • Grant the unprivileged account read, execute, and directory traverse rights.
  • Add specific write permissions to the /work, /temp and /logs directories in the $CATALINA_HOME directory.

For more information, see Web Application Server.

Example: Uniface and Tomcat Privileges on Unix

On Unix, similar mechanisms apply for running Tomcat under a lower privileged account.

The urouter process must be run using the local system account because it needs to start the userver process under specific user privileges, which requires local system account privileges. (The default account for the userver process is the userver user account.)

Related Links
  • State Management Mechanisms

    The stateless nature of the HTTP protocol means that Uniface server pages are stateless and must explicitly implement state management using one or more of the available state management mechanisms.
